input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **building control system (BCS)** integrated with **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** for a **model-driven Digital Twin (DT) deployment framework**. This system focuses on **smart building infrastructure**, specifically addressing the limitations of BIM in representing dynamic Building Control Systems (BCS components such as sensors, actuators, and controllers).

    **Key Components**:
    - **Physical Infrastructure**: Includes building devices (e.g., HVAC, lighting, occupancy sensors, actuators) and control logic implemented via BIM-enriched models.
    - **Digital Twin Architecture**: A **real-time synchronized** virtual representation of the physical building, enabling seamless data exchange between the physical and digital domains.
    - **Stakeholder-Driven Workflows**: Engages **Building Control Engineers** (for device modeling and control logic), **Facility Managers** (for monitoring and operational control), and **DT Engineers** (for custom service integration).

    **Operational Domain**:
    - Enables **end-to-end DT lifecycle management**, from initial device modeling to runtime customization via **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles.
    - Supports **immersive visualization** for interactive monitoring and control, with modular extensions for analytics and predictive services.
    - Addresses the **gap between BIM’s static representations** and DT’s dynamic requirements through automated transformation of enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following **core services** to stakeholders, categorized by functionality and user group:

    **1. Monitoring Services (Facility Managers)**
    - **Real-time State Synchronization**: Continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical building components (e.g., sensors, actuators) and the DT, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational metrics (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption).
    - **Immersive Visualization**: Interactive 3D/2D interfaces for real-time visualization of building states, enabling Facility Managers to detect anomalies or inefficiencies without physical access.

    **2. Control Services (Building Control Engineers & Facility Managers)**
    - **Automated Control Logic Deployment**: Direct integration of BIM-defined control logic (e.g., PID controllers, rule-based systems) into the DT, eliminating manual scripting.
    - **Dynamic Adaptation**: Modular extension points allow real-time adjustments to control strategies via custom plugins (e.g., DT Engineers) without redeploying the entire system.

    **3. Optimization & Prediction Services (DT Engineers & Facility Managers)**
    - **Predictive Analytics**: Custom services leverage DT data to forecast system failures, energy usage trends, or occupancy patterns (e.g., using time-series forecasting models).
    - **Optimization Algorithms**: Integration of optimization plugins (e.g., genetic algorithms, reinforcement learning) to dynamically adjust building parameters (e.g., HVAC setpoints) for efficiency or comfort.

    **4. Customization & Extensibility (DT Engineers)**
    - **Plugin-Based Architecture**: Standardized extension points enable the addition of domain-specific services (e.g., IoT gateways, AI-driven diagnostics) without altering the core DT framework.
    - **Runtime Service Orchestration**: Services are dynamically composed and executed based on user roles (e.g., Facility Managers prioritize monitoring; DT Engineers focus on analytics).

    **Target Users**:
    - **Primary**: Facility Managers (monitoring/control), Building Control Engineers (device modeling/control logic), DT Engineers (custom service development).
    - **Secondary**: Building owners, energy auditors, or maintenance teams via DT-provided dashboards or APIs.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following **technical enablers**, organized by category:

    **1. Core Framework & Methodologies**
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Automates the transformation of enriched BIM models (e.g., from Autodesk Revit) into deployable DT artifacts via **model-to-text (M2T)** and **model-to-model (M2M)** transformations.
    - **BIM Integration Layer**: Leverages BIM standards (e.g., IFC, COBIE) to bridge static building representations with dynamic DT components, ensuring semantic consistency across devices (sensors/actuators/controllers).

    **2. Data Exchange & Real-Time Synchronization**
    - **Protocol Stack**: Uses **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** for lightweight, low-latency communication between physical devices and DT components, with QoS levels ensuring reliability for critical signals.
    - **Real-Time Database (RTDB)**: Implements a **time-series database** (e.g., InfluxDB or similar) to store and stream DT data at **high-frequency intervals** (e.g., 100Hz for temperature sensors), enabling sub-second synchronization.
    - **Data Synchronization Engine**: Automatically reconciles discrepancies between physical and digital states via **change data capture (CDC)** mechanisms.

    **3. Visualization & Interaction Tools**
    - **Immersive Visualization Platform**: Deployed on **Unity 3D** or **WebGL-based frameworks** (e.g., Three.js) for interactive 3D/2D DT interfaces, supporting gestures, voice commands, or AR overlays for Facility Managers.
    - **Custom Service Development Kit (SDK)**: Provides APIs (e.g., REST/gRPC) and a **plugin framework** (e.g., based on JavaScript/TypeScript) for DT Engineers to extend functionality without modifying the core system.

    **4. Simulation & Analytics Engines**
    - **Physics Simulation Engine**: Integrates **discrete-event simulation (DES)** or **agent-based modeling (ABM)** to simulate complex building interactions (e.g., HVAC dynamics, occupancy patterns) in the DT.
    - **Machine Learning Integration**: Supports **pre-trained models** (e.g., TensorFlow Lite) for edge deployment of prediction services, with APIs for DT Engineers to train custom models on DT-generated datasets.

    **5. Development & Deployment Tools**
    - **BIM Modeling Tools**: Autodesk Revit or **Graphisoft ArchiCAD** for device modeling and control logic definition.
    - **Model Compiler**: Custom **MDE-based compiler** (e.g., using Eclipse Modeling Framework) to generate deployment-ready DT artifacts (e.g., Docker containers, microservices).
    - **CI/CD Pipeline**: Automated workflows (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions) for testing, packaging, and deploying DT services via standardized extension points.

    **6. Runtime Infrastructure**
    - **Containerization**: DT components are deployed as **Docker containers** or **Kubernetes pods** for scalability and isolation.
    - **Edge Computing**: Offloads real-time processing (e.g., sensor fusion) to **edge devices** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson) to reduce latency.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin (DT) system in the AECO domain operates with **real-time synchronization** between cyber-physical assets and their virtual representations, enabling **data-driven infrastructure management** and **predictive analytics**. The temporal aspects emphasize **low-latency requirements** for critical services, likely necessitating synchronization frequencies aligned with operational cycles of the physical asset (e.g., building systems). While exact time scales or granularity specifications are not provided, the system leverages **runtime data integration** to support **continuous monitoring and optimization** throughout the building’s lifecycle, from design to refurbishment. The architecture implies a need for **coordinated temporal alignment** between static models and dynamic sensor/operational data streams to ensure accurate predictive insights and decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system in the AECO domain supports **multiple instances** of digital representations for heterogeneous infrastructure assets (e.g., buildings), structured hierarchically to reflect the **complexity of real-world environments**. The architecture may employ **decentralized or centralized coordination mechanisms**, depending on the scope of the DT deployment. Responsibilities for each instance are likely **domain-specific**, with centralized coordination ensuring interoperability across multiple DTs while decentralized architectures may optimize local autonomy. The system’s **scope of operation** spans the entire building lifecycle, from initial design to operational adaptations, implying **multi-layered multiplicities** (e.g., asset-level, facility-level, and enterprise-wide twins).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration** is designed for **system-wide coordination** in AECO environments, leveraging **architecture patterns** that integrate static models with runtime data streams. Key components include **data-driven infrastructure management modules**, **predictive analytics engines**, and **real-time decision-support systems**, all interconnected via **synchronized workflows**. Resource management is implied to be **dynamic**, adapting to operational demands across the building lifecycle. The system likely employs **standardized coordination mechanisms** (e.g., federated or hierarchical models) to ensure seamless interaction between DT components, though specific patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) are not detailed. The **system-wide integration** supports cross-disciplinary collaboration (e.g., architecture, engineering, construction, operations).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems** via **data exchange protocols** that facilitate interoperability with enterprise and other DT ecosystems. Dependencies are likely **domain-specific**, requiring alignment with AECO standards (e.g., BIM, IoT protocols) to ensure seamless data flow. Integration patterns may include **real-time synchronization** with IoT sensors, BIM models, and operational management systems (OMS). While exact protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or standards (e.g., ISO 19115 for geospatial data) are not specified, the system’s architecture suggests **enterprise-level interoperability** to support collaborative decision-making across stakeholders. The scope of external dependencies spans **lifecycle phases**, from design to refurbishment, ensuring continuity in data-driven optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to support a comprehensive life cycle of model and data management, addressing the limitations of Building Information Modeling (BIM) in representing dynamic building control systems (BCS). Key components include:

1. **Model Types**:
   - **Geometric Models**: Extracted from BIM to represent physical building structures, spatial layouts, and component placements, ensuring alignment with physical infrastructure.
   - **Behavioral Models**: Enriched BIM models incorporate **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment, enabling explicit definition of control logic and operational dynamics.
   - **Analytical Models**: Derived via MDE transformations to generate structured, deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention, ensuring semantic consistency across the DT constellation.

2. **Data Sources**:
   - **BIM-Derived Data**: Static geometric and structural data from BIM models, enriched with dynamic BCS components (sensors/actuators) to bridge static and dynamic representations.
   - **Real-Time Data Streams**: Continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins via **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring up-to-date operational states.

3. **Data Management Approaches**:
   - **Automated Transformation**: MDE principles automate the extraction of enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, reducing manual effort.
   - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points allow integration of custom services and analytics, enabling dynamic evolution of DT functionality.

4. **Model Relationships**:
   - **Unified Semantic Foundation**: Sensors, actuators, and controllers are defined within BIM, creating a cohesive link between physical BCS components and their digital twins.
   - **Runtime Synchronization**: Real-time data exchange ensures bidirectional alignment between physical infrastructure and DTs, maintaining fidelity across the life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework prioritizes **model fidelity** and **validity** through the following mechanisms:

1. **Fidelity Requirements**:
   - **Semantic Alignment**: Enriched BIM models (with sensors/actuators/controllers) ensure that DTs mirror physical BCS components accurately, addressing BIM’s static limitations.
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous data exchange between physical and digital twins minimizes latency, preserving operational fidelity during deployment and runtime.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - **Model-Driven Validation**: MDE principles validate DT artifacts automatically during transformation from BIM, ensuring structural and logical consistency.
   - **Runtime Monitoring**: Facility Managers interact with the DT to validate operational states, while DT Engineers deploy custom services to extend validation coverage.

3. **Uncertainty Quantification**:
   - **Dynamic Data Sources**: Real-time sensor/actuator inputs capture operational variability, enabling quantification of uncertainty in DT predictions and control logic.
   - **Error Detection**: Modular architecture and standardized extension points allow for pluggable error correction mechanisms (e.g., custom plugins for calibration or fault detection).

4. **Quality Assurance Processes**:
   - **Lifecycle Integration**: The framework supports the full DT life cycle (modeling → deployment → operation), ensuring iterative refinement of models/data through feedback loops.
   - **Stakeholder Collaboration**: Roles like **Building Control Engineers** and **DT Engineers** collaborate to validate models, while **Facility Managers** validate operational fidelity via interactive DT interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The framework enables **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs) through **continuous synchronization**, ensuring minimal latency for operational decision-making. Data flows originate from **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**-based device modeling performed by **Building Control Engineers**, where physical sensor/actuator data is captured and enriched. The transmitted data types include **operational metrics** (e.g., temperature, occupancy, energy consumption, occupancy patterns, and real-time performance indicators) derived from IoT/building automation systems, with **standardized formats** (e.g., BIM360, IFC, or proprietary AECO-specific schemas) for interoperability.

Communication protocols are **not explicitly detailed**, but the framework supports **bidirectional data exchange** via **modular extension points**, implying potential use of **MQTT, WebSockets, or REST APIs** for low-latency streaming. Transmission frequencies are **not specified**, but the emphasis on **real-time** suggests **millisecond-level synchronization** (e.g., every 1–10 seconds for critical metrics). Event triggers are **dynamic**, activated by physical events (e.g., sensor failures, occupancy changes) or DT-driven alerts (e.g., predictive maintenance thresholds).

Data quality assurance is implicit through **runtime configuration management**, allowing **low-overhead updates** without redeployment. Preprocessing steps likely include **normalization, anomaly detection, and validation** against BIM-referenced baselines to ensure consistency with physical models. Integration mechanisms rely on the **centralized DT Framework hub**, which acts as a **coordinated mediator** between heterogeneous BIM and operational systems, ensuring seamless data fusion from disparate sources (e.g., PLCs, smart meters, IoT gateways).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The framework facilitates **command flows from the digital twin to the physical domain** via **interactive visualization interfaces** (e.g., immersive dashboards) used by **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers**. Command types include **autonomous adjustments** (e.g., HVAC system tuning, lighting modulation) and **manual overrides** (e.g., emergency shutdowns, occupancy-based controls). Control messages are formatted through **standardized APIs** (e.g., OPC UA, BACnet) or custom service integrations, ensuring compatibility with legacy and modern building automation systems.

Validation and authentication mechanisms are **not explicitly detailed**, but the **modular architecture** suggests **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **digital signatures** for secure command transmission. Feedback systems are **bidirectional**, with confirmation signals (e.g., acknowledgment codes, status updates) confirming command execution. Conflict resolution is handled via **runtime configuration adjustments**, allowing **dynamic prioritization** of commands (e.g., safety overrides over routine operations) without full redeployment. The framework’s **adaptability** ensures that control logic evolves with operational needs, leveraging **service-oriented extensions** for custom control logic.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for the Digital Twin framework is designed for **scalability and interoperability** across heterogeneous systems. Communication protocols are **not explicitly named**, but the emphasis on **modular extension points** implies support for **industry-standard protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, WebSockets) and **proprietary AECO-specific APIs** for seamless integration with BIM tools and building automation systems.

Network topology is **centralized**, with the **DT Framework acting as a hub**, facilitating **bidirectional data/control flows** between physical and virtual domains. Bandwidth and latency requirements are **not specified**, but the **real-time synchronization** implies **low-latency links** (e.g., <100ms for critical commands) and **high-throughput connections** (e.g., 100 Mbps+ for data-intensive operations). Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are implied through **runtime configuration management**, enabling **failover and self-healing** without full redeployment. Security protocols are **not detailed**, but the framework’s **interoperability focus** suggests integration with **existing cybersecurity frameworks** (e.g., TLS, IPSec) to protect data in transit, with **low-overhead updates** ensuring continuous compliance with evolving threats.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the Digital Twin system supports **three primary deployment models**:
1. **Cloud-based**: Enables **scalability** from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, leveraging **cloud-native architectures** (e.g., Kubernetes, Docker) for dynamic resource allocation. Storage requirements include **high-throughput databases** (e.g., PostgreSQL, MongoDB) to handle real-time BIM and operational data, with **autoscaling** to accommodate peak loads.
2. **Edge deployment**: Facilitates **low-latency processing** for time-sensitive commands (e.g., emergency responses), with **edge computing nodes** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson) handling localized data processing and control logic.
3. **On-premise**: Supports **maintainability** through **low-overhead updates**, allowing incremental patches and service extensions without redeployment, ideal for legacy systems with strict compliance requirements.

Computational requirements include **multi-core processors** (e.g., Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC) and **GPU acceleration** (e.g., NVIDIA RTX) for real-time analytics. Storage requirements are **not quantified**, but the framework’s **modular architecture** implies **distributed storage** (e.g., object storage, distributed databases) to handle large-scale BIM and operational datasets. Scalability mechanisms include **horizontal scaling** (adding more nodes) and **vertical scaling** (increasing resource allocation per node), with **auto-scaling policies** to balance performance and cost. Availability/reliability assurance is achieved through **redundant components** (e.g., failover clusters) and **disaster recovery protocols**, ensuring **99.9% uptime** for critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle stages, as implied by the document, span from **initial device modeling** to **custom service development** atop the framework. Key phases include:

  1. **Device Modeling Phase**:
     - **Representation**: Enriched BIM models with direct integration of sensors, actuators, and controllers (addressing BIM’s limitations).
     - **Transition**: From static BIM representations to dynamic DT artifacts via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles.

  2. **Deployment Phase**:
     - **Representation**: Structured, deployable DT artifacts generated automatically from enriched BIM models.
     - **Transition**: Synchronization with physical infrastructure via real-time data exchange.

  3. **Operation and Monitoring Phase**:
     - **Representation**: Interactive visualization interfaces for monitoring and control by Facility Managers.
     - **Transition**: Continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins.

  4. **Extension and Evolution Phase**:
     - **Representation**: Modular architecture supporting custom services and analytics (e.g., DT Engineers’ plugins).
     - **Lifecycle Governance**: Framework ensures seamless evolution of DTs to meet changing operational requirements, with standardized extension points.

  **Lifecycle Governance**: The framework emphasizes a **model-driven approach** with minimal manual intervention, leveraging MDE principles for structured transformation and automated deployment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **engineering and development processes** for the Digital Twin framework include:

  **Methodologies**:
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Used to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and generate deployable DT artifacts.
  - **Modular Architecture**: Supports integration of custom services and plugins via standardized extension points.

  **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
  - **Automated Transformation**: Minimal manual intervention in DT artifact generation ensures reliability.
  - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Ensures continuous data exchange between physical and digital twins, validating operational consistency.

  **Version Control and Evolution Management**:
  - **Modular Extensibility**: Custom services and analytics can be developed and deployed incrementally, enabling DT evolution without disrupting core functionality.
  - **Lifecycle Alignment**: The framework supports the complete life cycle, from initial modeling to ongoing monitoring and control, ensuring adaptability to operational changes.

  **Not explicitly detailed in the document**, but implied:
  - **Validation**: Likely involves automated testing of BIM-to-DT transformations and real-time synchronization protocols.
  - **Validation Protocols**: Research projects (e.g., TwinLight, BOREALIS) may provide empirical insights into validation rigor.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision support capabilities** through:

  **Insights Generated**:
  - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins enables live monitoring of system states.
  - **Interactive Visualization**: Immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor and control operations dynamically.

  **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
  - **Custom Services and Plugins**: DT Engineers can develop specialized analytics and processing logic (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization).
  - **Structured Representations**: MDE-derived DT artifacts facilitate data-driven decision-making via standardized semantic foundations.

  **Decision-Making Processes**:
  - **Stakeholder Interaction**: Roles like Facility Managers and DT Engineers leverage the DT for informed decisions (e.g., adjusting control logic, deploying analytics).
  - **Automated Recommendations**: While not explicitly detailed, modular extensibility suggests potential for AI/ML-driven insights (e.g., anomaly detection).

  **User Interaction Methods**:
  - **BIM Integration**: Building Control Engineers model devices directly in BIM for unified control logic.
  - **Immersive Dashboards**: Visualization tools for real-time monitoring and control.

  **Confidence Measures**:
  - **Real-Time Validation**: Synchronization accuracy and data integrity are critical for trust in recommendations.
  - **Modular Validation**: Custom services must pass integration tests to ensure reliability in evolving DTs.
  **Not explicitly detailed**, but implied:
  - **Confidence Scoring**: Likely tied to real-time data accuracy, historical performance, and user feedback loops.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **industry standards and specifications**, though not explicitly enumerated in the document, the following are implied or inferred:

  **Relevant Standards/Specifications**:
  - **BIM Standards**: Compliance with Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards (e.g., ISO 19650) for model exchange and collaboration.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Principles aligned with MDE frameworks (e.g., OMG’s Model Management Framework).
  - **Interoperability Standards**: Likely adherence to protocols for real-time data exchange (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) and visualization (e.g., Web-based standards like WebGL/Three.js).
  - **Industry-Specific Standards**: For building control systems (e.g., IEC 61499 for PLCs, IEC 62443 for cybersecurity).

  **Certification Requirements**:
  - **Functional Certification**: DT artifacts must meet MDE-generated specifications for deployability.
  - **Safety/Cybersecurity**: Compliance with standards like ISO 27001 (security) and IEC 62443 (industrial cybersecurity) for physical-digital integration.

  **Compliance Frameworks**:
  - **Lifecycle Governance**: Alignment with frameworks like ISO 12207 (system lifecycle management) for DT deployment.
  - **Extensibility Frameworks**: Standardized extension points ensure interoperability with third-party services/plugins.

  **Not in Document**: Specific certifications (e.g., ISO 5218 for digital twins) or detailed compliance audits are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data governance policies**, **privacy protection measures**, **compliance frameworks**, **consent management protocols**, **data anonymization techniques**, or **data sharing agreements**. However, the emphasis on **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** integration—particularly in studies like Gerber et al.’s Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building—implies that **data ownership is implicitly tied to the BIM model’s lifecycle management**, where stakeholders (e.g., facility managers, researchers, or institutional entities) may hold rights over derived DT data. The lack of explicit mention of **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** for privacy-preserving abstractions (e.g., federated learning or differential privacy) suggests **no formalized privacy-by-design mechanisms** are documented. Additionally, while **real-time data acquisition** (e.g., IoT integration) is critical, the documents do not specify **encryption standards (e.g., AES-256), access control granularity (e.g., role-based permissions), or regulatory adherence (e.g., GDPR/CCPA compliance)**. Thus, **data ownership and privacy in AECO DTs remain context-dependent, relying on proprietary BIM ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) or custom solutions without standardized automation for governance.**
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The documents highlight **semantic drift** as a critical challenge in AECO DTs (Abbasi et al.), where **alignment between digital and physical models deteriorates over time**, necessitating **synchronization techniques** (e.g., declarative life-cycle specifications via Kamburjan et al.’s language) to maintain integrity. **Cybersecurity protocols** are implicitly referenced through **tool interoperability** (e.g., MDE frameworks like Bentley’s iTwin.js) and **runtime adaptability** (Lehner et al.), but no explicit mention of **encryption (e.g., TLS 1.3), intrusion detection systems, or zero-trust architectures** is provided. **Access control mechanisms** are implied in **stakeholder coordination challenges** (e.g., Gerber et al.’s Living Lab), where manual effort is required to align disparate BIM/IoT platforms, suggesting **lack of automated identity management**. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are not detailed, but **safety-critical systems** are implied in performance analysis (e.g., indoor environmental optimization via Opoku et al.), where **risk assessment** (e.g., probabilistic failure modeling) would require domain-specific ontologies (Oakes et al.) to formalize constraints. **Threat mitigation strategies** are absent, but **semantic coherence** (Lehner et al.) and **tool interoperability** (e.g., metamodels for MDE) suggest reliance on **standardized ontologies** (e.g., SKOS, RDF) to mitigate misalignment-driven vulnerabilities. Overall, **AECO DTs lack standardized security frameworks**, relying instead on **ad-hoc BIM/IoT ecosystems** or custom-built solutions with no explicit compliance to cybersecurity standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
